paper_title: Probing the electron Yukawa coupling via resonant Higgs boson production at FCC-ee via \texorpdfstring$e^+e^- \to H \to WW^*$

perturbations.why_wrong: The units for luminosity are conventionally defined as inverse area per unit time. Changing the exponent from -2 to -1 makes the units cm/s, which is a velocity, not a luminosity flux.

Integrated luminosity is measured in inverse cross-section units. Removing the negative sign changes the unit from inverse femtobarns to femtobarns, which is a unit of area/cross-section, creating a dimensional inconsistency with the definition of integrated luminosity.

The standard unit for integrated luminosity at future colliders is inverse attobarns (ab$^{-1}$). Changing the exponent to -2 is inconsistent with the standard definition of integrated luminosity used throughout the rest of the document.
The definition of the unit for integrated luminosity must be inverse area. Squaring the inverse area unit (fb$^{-2}$) is mathematically inconsistent with the product of luminosity and time.

The title and the abstract specify the analysis of the 'lepton-plus-jets' final state. While $ZZ^*$ can produce leptons and jets, the subsequent text specifically discusses $W$ boson branching fractions and the $WW^*$ signal topology. Changing the signal to $ZZ^*$ in the title creates a contradiction with the detailed analysis sections.

The paper describes the FCC-ee, which is an electron-positron collider. Changing the initial state to $e^-e^-$ (electron-electron) is inconsistent with the $s$-channel Higgs production mechanism ($e^+e^- \to H$) described in the abstract.

The abstract states the goal is to study $s$-channel Higgs production. Since the Higgs mass is known to be approximately 125 GeV, setting the center-of-mass energy to 91 GeV (the Z-pole) would make resonant Higgs production impossible, contradicting the paper's primary objective.

The abstract explicitly mentions the 'electron Yukawa coupling'. $y_b$ refers to the bottom quark Yukawa coupling. This creates an internal inconsistency between the text description and the symbol used.

The text immediately following this formula describes the final state as 'lepton-plus-jets'. The perturbed formula shows a fully leptonic decay ($\ell\nu\ell\nu$), which contradicts the 'plus-jets' ($jj$) description.

The context describes the decay into 'lepton-plus-jets' via $W$ bosons. Changing the intermediate state to $ZZ^*$ while keeping the description of $W$ boson decays creates a physics inconsistency in the signal chain definition.

The text defines the process as decaying into dileptons and then immediately attempts to specify the flavor content; using a 'not equal' sign contradicts the subsequent inclusion of electrons in the signal definition.

In the context of $W^\pm$ decays to leptons, the lepton charge must match the $W$ boson charge. Changing the superscript to $\mp$ while the electron is $\pm$ creates an inconsistency in the charge conservation description for the $WW^*$ system.
The text describes $W$ decays to leptons ($\ell^\pm$). Using $\tau^\mp$ suggests a charge conjugation that is inconsistent with the $W^\pm$ decay products mentioned in the same sentence.

The notation $jj$ represents two jets from a $W$ decay. Changing it to $j^2$ is notationally inconsistent with standard experimental physics representations of dijet final states used elsewhere in the paper.

The cross section is directly tied to the beam energy spread (4.1 MeV) and the resulting significance (2.0 sigma). Doubling the cross section while keeping the luminosity and significance constant creates a mathematical inconsistency in the sensitivity calculation.
Luminosity is measured in inverse area (ab$^{-1}$). Changing the exponent to $+1$ (ab$^1$) is dimensionally incorrect for integrated luminosity.

An upper limit at a confidence level must use the 'less than or equal to' ($\lesssim$) operator. Flipping it to 'greater than or equal to' ($\gtrsim$) describes a lower bound, which contradicts the definition of an exclusion limit.

The text cites the LHC discovery papers (Aad et al., Chatrchyan et al.) which are well-known to have discovered the Higgs at 125 GeV; 91 GeV is the mass of the Z boson, creating a factual conflict with the cited discovery of the scalar Higgs.
The numerical value is calculated from $m_e$ (0.511 MeV) and $v$ (246.22 GeV). The perturbed value is exactly double the correct result, making the equation internally inconsistent.

The sentence defines the electron Yukawa coupling ($y_e$). Replacing the electron mass ($m_e$) with the Z-boson mass ($m_Z$) in the definition of the electron Yukawa coupling is physically and terminologically incorrect.
